title: North Korea registers Choe Hyon destroyer with IMO, signals intent for open-water operations
summary: ISW analysis revealed North Korea registered its new 5,000-ton Choe Hyon-class guided missile destroyer with the International Maritime Organization, the first formal warship registration by Pyongyang, signaling preparation for operations beyond coastal waters.
contents:
## North Korea Prepares Green-Water Naval Capability

### Key Development
The Institute for the Study of War (ISW) reported that North Korea registered the Choe Hyon guided missile destroyer with the UN International Maritime Organization (IMO) — the first time Pyongyang formally registered a major surface combatant.

### Vessel Capabilities
- The Choe Hyon-class displaces approximately 5,000 tons
- Armed with an 88-cell vertical launch system (VLS) for cruise missiles
- Equipped with phased-array radar panels on four faces providing 360-degree coverage
- Tested both land-attack cruise missiles and a Kh-35 variant anti-ship missile
- Primarily functions as a mobile nuclear-capable strike platform rather than a traditional surface warfare ship

### Fleet Expansion Plan
- Kim Jong Un directed construction of two destroyers annually through 2030
- Target fleet size: 12 oceangoing surface combatants
- The second vessel, Kanggeon, has entered service
- A third hull shows active construction at Nampo shipyard

### Strategic Implications
- IMO registration signals intent for international waters operations
- The Northern Limit Line (NLL) in the Yellow Sea faces increased pressure
- South Korea must redistribute surveillance assets to counter the seaborne missile threat
- The destroyer fleet diversifies North Korea's nuclear delivery platforms

### Source Attribution
ISW Korean Peninsula Update (April 22, 2026), 38 North quarterly analysis, Yonhap reporting.
